summary: I had a young recruiter come out from Green Bay who invited me to go along to Seymour to help with recruiting. I had met him at different functions before, and he encouraged me to wear my old World War II parachute jump suit and beret for the event. It was a good experience talking to high school students about history and the military.

I went to Seymour, Wisconsin, with a recruiter to speak to senior high school students about my military experiences. It was just before Christmas, and I shared stories about World War II, including pictures from the 1936 Olympics. The students were very interested and even sent me a thank-you letter afterward.

I was drafted just before Pearl Harbor and trained during the buildup to World War II. I served in the Medical Corps and later joined the airborne troops, going through tough parachute training at Fort Benning in 1943. The war was tough, but I believed in the cause and would do it again if I had to.

I have a picture from the 1936 Summer Olympics showing Jesse Owens' remarkable performance that even baffled Hitler. It was one of the better historical images I shared with students during a recruiting talk in Seymour. That moment symbolized a powerful story of triumph during a tense time.

Jesse Owens was a standout athlete at the 1936 Olympics, and I had a picture of him that I showed to students during a recruiting event. His success was a memorable example of overcoming adversity, especially given the political climate with Hitler watching. It was a story that resonated well with the young people I spoke to.

I was inducted into the Army at Fort Sheridan, Illinois, in early December 1941, just days before Pearl Harbor. We stayed there for about a week, completing physicals and receiving our uniforms before moving on to Camp Barkeley, Texas. Fort Sheridan was the starting point of my military journey.

After Fort Sheridan, I was sent to Camp Barkeley, Texas, near Abilene, which was a new medical training center expanding rapidly. I completed my basic medical training there and stayed on as cadre for several training cycles. It was also where I first saw a notice recruiting for airborne troops, which changed my path.

Most of the men I trained with at Camp Barkeley were sent to Australia after completing basic training. I stayed behind as cadre, but about four-fifths of my original group went there as part of the war effort. It was a common destination for troops preparing to enter the Pacific theater.

I was at Camp Barkeley, Texas, when Pearl Harbor was attacked on December 7, 1941. The news changed everything overnight, and the military ramped up its efforts. We had just arrived at Camp Barkeley and were training when the attack happened, which made the war feel very real and urgent.

I trained and served in the United States Army Medical Corps during World War II. Our training was thorough, focusing on first aid and hospital work, and we carried no weapons, only Red Cross armbands. I stayed on as cadre for several training cycles, helping prepare new medics for service.

As a medic in the Army Medical Corps, I wore a Red Cross band on my arm to identify myself as non-combatant. We carried no weapons and focused on providing medical aid during training and combat. The Red Cross symbol was an important part of our role and identity.

While training at Camp Barkeley, the 45th Infantry Division, known as the Thunderbird Division, was also there preparing to go overseas. This National Guard division was primarily made up of Mexican and Indian soldiers from Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico. A friend of mine who was captured at Anzio served with them and told me many of those men were killed or captured.

I spent a significant part of my training in Texas, at Camp Barkeley near Abilene. Texas was also home to many soldiers in the 45th Infantry Division, who were primarily Mexican and Indian. Later, recruits came from other Texas camps like Fort Hood when I was selected for airborne training.

Oklahoma was one of the states where many soldiers in the 45th Infantry Division came from. This division, which trained at Camp Barkeley, included a large number of Mexican and Indian soldiers from Oklahoma, Texas, and New Mexico. It was part of the National Guard units preparing for overseas deployment.

New Mexico was among the states contributing soldiers to the 45th Infantry Division, which trained at Camp Barkeley. The division was mostly composed of Mexican and Indian soldiers from Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico. They were a National Guard unit preparing for combat overseas.

A friend of mine who served with the 45th Infantry Division was captured at Anzio during the war. He told me that many of the Mexican and Indian soldiers in his unit were either killed or captured there. Anzio was a tough battle and a significant event for those soldiers.

I served as a medic in the 101st Airborne Division, where each platoon had three medics. The airborne training was tough but rewarding, and I was proud to be part of such a highly trained unit. The 101st was smaller than typical infantry units but very effective in combat.

I went to Fort Benning, Georgia, in early 1943 for parachute training to join the airborne forces. The training was rigorous and selective, with only about fifty out of two hundred fifty candidates accepted. Fort Benning was where I transitioned from medical training to becoming a paratrooper.

Georgia was where Fort Benning is located, and that's where I completed my parachute training in early 1943. The training was tough and designed to prepare us for airborne combat. Fort Benning was a key site for transforming soldiers into paratroopers during the war.

By the time I was training for airborne, the 82nd Airborne Division was already fighting overseas in North Africa and Sicily. Their success was well known and inspired many of us to volunteer for airborne duty. The 82nd set the standard for airborne operations during the war.

The 82nd Airborne Division was involved in the North Africa Campaign before I joined airborne training. Their efforts there were part of the early American offensive in World War II. Hearing about their success motivated many of us to volunteer for airborne service.

The 82nd Airborne Division also took part in the Sicily Campaign, which was a critical phase of the war in Europe. Their airborne operations there were challenging but effective. This campaign was part of the reason airborne units gained such a strong reputation.

While I was at Camp Barkeley, the 90th Infantry Division arrived to begin their training as the 45th moved out. The 90th was another infantry division preparing for combat overseas. I was there about a year, so I saw several units come and go during that time.

Some of the men who came to airborne recruitment at Camp Barkeley came from Fort Hood, Texas. Fort Hood was one of the many training camps in Texas contributing soldiers to the airborne forces. It was part of the broader military training network during the war.

Most of the men volunteering for airborne training were born around 1920 and grew up during the Great Depression and drought. Those hard times shaped our generation and our outlook on service and sacrifice. It was a tough era that made us determined to do our part in the war.

I mentioned John Steinbeck when talking about the era we grew up in, especially his book The Grapes of Wrath, which captured the struggles of that time. Steinbeck's work resonated with many of us who lived through the Great Depression and drought. It helped frame the context of our youth before the war.

The Grapes of Wrath was a book that really spoke to the hardships of the Great Depression and drought that shaped my generation. I referenced it when discussing the background of the men who joined the military in World War II. It was a powerful depiction of the struggles many faced before the war.

The Works Progress Administration was one of the New Deal programs during the Great Depression that helped many people, including those in my generation. While I didn't work directly for them, their efforts were part of the backdrop of the era I grew up in. It was a time of hardship but also of government programs trying to help.

The Civilian Conservation Corps was another New Deal program during the Great Depression that aimed to provide jobs and improve the country. Like the Works Progress Administration, it set the scene for the generation that went on to fight in World War II. These programs helped many families survive tough times.

During the airborne recruitment at Camp Barkeley, men came from all branches, including the United States Army Air Forces. The airborne was looking for volunteers from infantry, artillery, air force, and others. It was a diverse group eager to join the tough airborne units.

While I didn't train specifically in the Deep South, Fort Benning, Georgia, where I did my parachute training, is part of that region. The Deep South had many military installations that were crucial for training troops during World War II. It was a significant area for preparing soldiers for combat.
